---
title: "M3. Estimation theory: least squares, MLE, Fisher information"
subtitle: "The Cramér–Rao bound and the best you can possibly do"
author: Robert Waddy
date: last-modified
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

```{=html}
<div class="chapter-header-meta">
  <strong>Part I — Measurement &amp; Uncertainty</strong> &middot; Module M3
  &middot; Prerequisite: M1 (probability), M2 (error propagation)
</div>
```

*Fitting data is not just arithmetic — it is inference. This module develops the statistical language that asks: given this dataset, what is the best estimate of my parameter, and how well can any unbiased estimator do?  The answer is the **Cramér–Rao bound**, and understanding it is what separates an experiment designed to learn something from one designed to look busy.*

---

## Core {#sec-fisher-core}

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal" icon="false"}
### Core

#### Estimators

An **estimator** $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ is any function of the data $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_N)$ used to infer a parameter $\theta$.  Three properties matter:

- **Bias:** $B(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \theta$.  An estimator is *unbiased* if $B = 0$.
- **Consistency:** $\hat\theta \to \theta$ in probability as $N \to \infty$.
- **Efficiency:** how close $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)$ is to the theoretical minimum.

The sample mean $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ is the canonical example: unbiased ($\mathbb{E}[\bar x] = \mu$) and consistent.

#### Least squares as a special case of MLE

For data $y_i = f(x_i; \theta) + \varepsilon_i$ with independent Gaussian noise $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_i^2)$, the log-likelihood is

$$
\ln \mathcal{L}(\theta; \mathbf{y}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{[y_i - f(x_i;\theta)]^2}{\sigma_i^2} + \text{const}.
$$ {#eq-log-likelihood-gaussian}

Maximising @eq-log-likelihood-gaussian is identical to minimising the **weighted sum of squares**

$$
\chi^2(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{[y_i - f(x_i;\theta)]^2}{\sigma_i^2}.
$$ {#eq-chi2}

Ordinary (unweighted) least squares arises when all $\sigma_i$ are equal.  **Least squares is MLE for Gaussian errors** — no more, no less.

#### The likelihood function

The **likelihood** $\mathcal{L}(\theta; \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}|\theta)$ is the probability of the observed data, viewed as a function of the parameter.  For $N$ i.i.d. observations from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$,

$$
\ln \mathcal{L}(\mu) = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2}(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 + \text{const},
$$ {#eq-gaussian-logL}

a parabola in $\mu$ with curvature $N/\sigma^2$.  The MLE is $\hat\mu = \bar x$, and the width of the likelihood encodes the uncertainty — as we will quantify next.

:::

---

## Going deeper {#sec-fisher-deeper}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal" icon="false"}
### Going deeper

#### Fisher information

The **Fisher information** $I(\theta)$ measures how much information the data carry about $\theta$:

$$
I(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p(X|\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]
           = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(X|\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right].
$$ {#eq-fisher-information}

The second form (valid under regularity conditions) is usually easier to compute: it is the expected curvature of the log-likelihood.  High curvature $\Leftrightarrow$ the data are sensitive to $\theta$ $\Leftrightarrow$ the parameter is well-determined.

**Example (Gaussian, known $\sigma$):**  $\ln p(x|\mu) = -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \text{const}$, so $\partial^2/\partial\mu^2 = -1/\sigma^2$ and

$$
I(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}.
$$ {#eq-fisher-gaussian}

For $N$ i.i.d. samples, $I_N(\mu) = N/\sigma^2$ — information scales linearly with sample size.

#### The Cramér–Rao bound

For any **unbiased** estimator $\hat\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}.}
$$ {#eq-crb}

This is the **Cramér–Rao bound (CRB)**.  No unbiased estimator can have variance below $1/I(\theta)$, regardless of how clever you are.  An estimator achieving equality is called **efficient**.

The MLE $\hat\mu = \bar x$ for the Gaussian achieves $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\mu) = \sigma^2/N = 1/I_N$, so the sample mean is efficient.

#### Why this matters for experiment design

The CRB tells you the best you can ever do.  If your current estimator has $\operatorname{Var} \gg 1/I$, you are leaving information on the table — a better estimator or analysis exists.  If you are already at the CRB, buying more sensitivity requires collecting more data or reducing $\sigma$ (i.e., improving the apparatus).

The ratio $e(\hat\theta) = 1 / [I(\theta)\,\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)] \in (0, 1]$ is the **efficiency**.  Designing an experiment means choosing the observable $X$ and the estimator $\hat\theta$ to maximise $I(\theta)$ for the quantity you care about.

#### Profile likelihoods and nuisance parameters

When the model has nuisance parameters $\bm{\eta}$ alongside the parameter of interest $\theta$, the **profile likelihood** is

$$
\mathcal{L}_P(\theta) = \max_{\bm{\eta}} \mathcal{L}(\theta, \bm{\eta}),
$$

obtained by fixing $\theta$ and maximising over $\bm{\eta}$.  Wilks' theorem states that $-2\Delta\ln\mathcal{L}_P$ is asymptotically $\chi^2$ distributed with one degree of freedom — enabling likelihood-ratio confidence intervals without knowing the distribution of $\hat\theta$ analytically.

:::

---

## Reproducible figure: likelihood profile and the CRB {#sec-fisher-figure}

The figure below is generated from simulated data by the Python script
[`figures/src/likelihood_crb.py`](../../figures/src/likelihood_crb.py),
using the dataset [`data/sample_calibration.csv`](../../data/sample_calibration.csv).
Everything is reproducible: fixed seed, pinned dependencies, no manual steps.

```{python}
#| label: fig-likelihood-crb
#| fig-cap: "Log-likelihood profile for the mean of $N=10$ Gaussian measurements (known $\\sigma=1$). The MLE $\\hat{\\mu}$ is marked; the green band is the Cramér–Rao bound $\\pm\\sqrt{\\sigma^2/N}$. The $\\Delta\\ln\\mathcal{L} = -\\tfrac{1}{2}$ line defines the 1$\\sigma$ confidence interval from the likelihood."
#| echo: true

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pathlib

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "serif", "font.size": 10,
    "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
})

mu_true, sigma_known, N = 5.0, 1.0, 10
data = rng.normal(mu_true, sigma_known, N)
mu_hat = data.mean()

mu_grid = np.linspace(2.5, 7.5, 500)
log_L = np.array([-0.5 * np.sum(((data - mu) / sigma_known)**2) for mu in mu_grid])
log_L -= log_L.max()

crb_sigma = sigma_known / np.sqrt(N)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 3.6))
ax.plot(mu_grid, log_L, color="#1a4a7a",
        label=r"$\ln \mathcal{L}(\mu;\,\mathbf{x})$")
ax.axhline(-0.5, color="#1a4a7a", lw=0.8, ls="--", alpha=0.6)
ax.text(7.4, -0.46, r"$\Delta\ln\mathcal{L} = -1/2$",
        ha="right", fontsize=8, color="#555")
ax.axvline(mu_hat, color="#c75000", lw=1.2, ls=":",
           label=fr"MLE $\hat{{\mu}}={mu_hat:.2f}$")
ax.axvspan(mu_hat - crb_sigma, mu_hat + crb_sigma, color="#2e7d32",
           alpha=0.12,
           label=fr"CRB: $\sigma_\mathrm{{CRB}}={crb_sigma:.3f}$")
ax.axvline(mu_true, color="#888", lw=0.9, ls="-.",
           label=fr"True $\mu_0={mu_true}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"Normalised $\ln\mathcal{L}$")
ax.set_ylim(-3.5, 0.3)
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

@fig-likelihood-crb demonstrates a key result: the width of the likelihood at the $\Delta\ln\mathcal{L}=-\frac{1}{2}$ level equals the CRB exactly for the Gaussian mean problem.  This is not a coincidence — it follows from @eq-gaussian-logL and @eq-crb.

The $\chi^2$-fit-with-residuals companion figure (demonstrating the full weighted least-squares pipeline on real calibration data) will appear in Module M4.

---

## Bench {#sec-fisher-bench}

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal" icon="false"}
### Bench Notes for this module

- [Fisher information & the Cramér–Rao bound on one page](../../bench-notes/exact-constraint-design.qmd) *(placeholder — CRB Bench Note to be added)*
- [Reading a fit: residuals, chi-squared_red, and covariance](../../bench-notes/index.qmd)

:::

---

## Key references {#sec-fisher-refs}

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal" icon="false"}
### Key references

- @cowan1998statistical — Chapter 6 (maximum likelihood) and Chapter 7 (least squares).
- @kayEstimationTheory — the definitive graduate reference on Fisher information and the CRB.
- @siviaSkilling2006 — Bayesian perspective and comparison with MLE.
- @bevingtonRobinson2003 — practical worked examples with real datasets.
- @jcgm2008gum — the metrological standard for uncertainty propagation.

:::

---

## Exercises {#sec-fisher-exercises .unnumbered}

1. For $N$ i.i.d. observations from a Poisson distribution $\text{Po}(\lambda)$, compute $I(\lambda)$ and verify that the sample mean achieves the CRB.
2. Show that the biased estimator $\hat\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar x)^2$ of the variance has lower MSE than the unbiased estimator $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2$ when $N$ is small.  What does this tell you about the trade-off between bias and variance?
3. Using the dataset in `data/sample_calibration.csv`, perform a weighted linear fit and verify that your parameter uncertainties saturate the CRB.  Plot the residuals.
4. *(Going deeper)* Derive the profile likelihood for a linear model $y = a x + b$ when only $b$ is the parameter of interest and $a$ is a nuisance parameter.  Show that the resulting confidence interval on $b$ is wider than the naive interval that ignores $a$.
